web.title: The Anthology of a Creative Developer: A 2026 Portfolio

web.markdown:
_This is a submission for the [New Year, New You Portfolio Challenge Presented by Google AI](https://dev.to/challenges/new-year-new-you-google-ai-2025-12-31)_ (just check my github, not for prizes 😅)

## The Prologue: Beyond the "Sectioned Resume"

Most portfolios feel like a list of ingredients. For 2026, I wanted mine to be the **complete meal.**

I’m **Nitish Kumar**, an undergraduate engineering student and creative developer. I’ve always felt that code is just another way of storytelling—so I ditched the typical "Hero-About-Projects" grid. Instead, I built a **narrative experience** that reflects how I think, build, and observe the world.

* * *

## The Gallery

I built this to be "authored"—a stroll through a digital gallery rather than a scroll through a resume.

🔗 **Explore the Story:** [https://nitishkr.fun](https://nitishkr.fun/)

* * *

## The Craft (How I Built It)

If this portfolio were a book, here is how I bound the pages:

### **The Technical Spine**

- **Next.js (App Router):** The foundation that holds the chapters together.
- **TypeScript:** My editor, ensuring every "sentence" (and variable) makes sense.
- **GSAP:** The cinematography—adding subtle, intentional motion that guides the eye.
- **Tailwind CSS:** For the editorial aesthetic and spacing.
- **Cloud Run:** My printing press, keeping everything fast and accessible.

### **The Editorial Vision**

I treated the design like a high-end magazine. Each section is a chapter:

1. **Identity:** The "Who" and the mindset.
2. **The Workshop:** Skills and tools as a craftsman’s kit.
3. **The Lab:** Projects as experiments, not just finished products.
4. **The Lens:** Using photography as a reflection of how I observe UI/UX in the real world.

### **Google AI: The Creative Director**

Google AI wasn't a "generator" for me; it was a **thinking partner**. I used it to:

- **Refine the Copy:** Helping me transition from dry technical descriptions to narrative-driven prose.
- **Accessibility Review:** Ensuring the "story" remains readable for everyone.
- **Logic Soundboarding:** Using AI to brainstorm the best way to structure a chapter-based layout.

* * *

## The "Director's Cut" (What I'm Proud Of)

- **Story-Driven Flow:** The site feels like a single, continuous conversation.
- **Intentional Dark Mode:** Designed from the ground up to feel like a premium "night-time" reading experience.
- **The Power of Restraint:** Knowing when to stop adding features and start refining the white space.
- **Photography Integration:** My photos aren't just "assets"—they are windows into my perspective as a designer.

* * *

## Closing Thoughts

This portfolio is my way of proving that engineering is an art form. It’s about more than just shipping code; it’s about crafting experiences that linger.

Thanks for checking it out, and **Happy New Year!**

# ‘Engineer’ is so 2025. In AI land, everyone’s a ‘builder’ now

From Big Tech to startups, Silicon Valley has a new job title for people who use AI agents to turn ideas into products.

![A smiling woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing gold hoop earrings, a white top, a black blazer, and a delicate gold necklace.](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_4a85id676p2vl41rs6442rtr64/-C100.00%25x66.67%25%2C0.00%25%2C7.55%25-S3840x3840-FPNG)

By [Rya Jetha](https://sfstandard.com/author/rya-jetha/)Tech Culture Reporter

Published Mar. 5, 2026•6:00am

Say goodbye to software developers — and hello to builders.

It used to be that coders spent most of their time, well, writing code. But with the new wave of sophisticated AI coding tools, engineers increasingly spend their days giving agents instructions in plain English and reviewing what the machines spit out.

​​That shift is scrambling the traditional org chart in tech. Engineers are being asked to think more like product managers. And product managers are now expected to be technical. Companies are mandating [“AI fluency”](https://sfstandard.com/2025/07/24/layoffs-firing-ai-engineers-tech-white-collar-jobs/) across the workforce, with employees sketching out ideas in the morning and spinning up functional prototypes by lunch.

ADVERTISEMENT

Teams at tech companies used to be organized like assembly lines: Product managers wrote specs, designers mocked things up, and engineers implemented the code. But now that AI tools can propose architectures and execute sophisticated projects with no human guidance, that system is being deprecated. A person with the right instincts and prompts can move from idea to working product in hours.

Silicon Valley is responding by inventing a new job.

“Builder” is meant to signal someone who can spot a problem, decide how to solve it, and use AI to bring the solution to life. Across startups and big tech companies alike, employees are swapping “software engineer” and “product manager” for the broader, more assertive title.

The job is already showing up on LinkedIn. Some [Meta product managers (opens in new tab)](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-pms-ai-builders-tech-industry-2026-2) are calling themselves “AI builders” — though their official job title remains the same — signaling that they, too, are using AI coding tools to build software at the company.

More about [Labor](https://sfstandard.com/tag/labor/)

- [![](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_1fbrbq0ltp2up8un7eg8rb276a/-S3840x2561-FPNG)](https://sfstandard.com/2026/03/18/sf-labor-leaders-chavez-movement-bigger-one-man/)
[**SF labor leaders on Chavez: ‘The movement is bigger than one man’**](https://sfstandard.com/2026/03/18/sf-labor-leaders-chavez-movement-bigger-one-man/)

- [![A group of people wearing purple shirts stand on a sidewalk outside a building, holding signs that say "SEIU Unfair Labor Practice On Strike."](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_bjgkfv1gmh615ct0vchm88ln5g/-C100.00%25x88.54%25%2C0.00%25%2C9.84%25-S2048x1365-FPNG)](https://sfstandard.com/2026/02/27/san-francisco-superior-court-clerks-strike/)
[**Outside the strike zone: SF courts are a mess as clerks picket**](https://sfstandard.com/2026/02/27/san-francisco-superior-court-clerks-strike/)

- [![A computer keyboard with all keys labeled "AI" instead of their usual letters or symbols.](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_od30oh9h6l25d37jomcb13ga5i/-S3840x2560-FPNG)](https://sfstandard.com/2026/02/19/ai-writes-code-now-s-left-software-engineers/)
[**AI writes the code now. What’s left for software engineers?**](https://sfstandard.com/2026/02/19/ai-writes-code-now-s-left-software-engineers/)


“Building has always been my passion. AI gave me the ability to turn ideas into real, working apps. That changed everything,” Meta product manager Jeremie Guedj, who now identifies as an “AI builder,” [wrote on LinkedIn (opens in new tab)](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/jeremieguedj_i-still-cant-believe-im-writing-this-as-activity-7426699982641979392-aqEf?utm_source=social_share_send&utm_medium=member_desktop_web&rcm=ACoAAC-2DvwBRIvdBfCrasIykFHf1AvQQDk829E) last month.

The democratization of coding has been so dramatic that software engineer Boris Cherny, who created Anthropic’s AI coding tool Claude Code, has predicted the demise of his own profession.

“Today coding is practically solved,” Cherny said last month on the [Y Combinator podcast (opens in new tab)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PQU9o_5rHC4), referring to the fact that AI tools have become so good that most engineers [rarely write code anymore](https://sfstandard.com/2026/02/19/ai-writes-code-now-s-left-software-engineers/). “We’re going to start to see the title of ‘software engineer’ go away. It’s just going to be ‘builder’ or ‘product manager.’”

![A man with graying hair and beard wears a blue shirt and gray hoodie, speaking with a hand gesture while seated against a black backdrop with text.](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_mddi1mitqd00t9nekoqvc90a51/-S3840x2561-FPNG)

Khan Academy founder Sal Khan predicted last week that everyone is going to learn how to be a builder. \| Source: Amanda Andrade-Rhoades

Last year, LinkedIn launched what it calls a “full stack builder” program. It trains employees, regardless of job title, to pick up skills that were traditionally split across teams.

“The full stack builder takes what would’ve been days or weeks as a conveyor belt between design, product, engineering … and gives it to an individual with these \[AI\] tools,” Aneesh Raman, LinkedIn’s chief economic opportunity officer, said at last week’s [Leading With AI event hosted by The Standard and Charter (opens in new tab)](https://www.charterworks.com/events/ai-summit/2026/sf/).

- [YouTube (opens in new tab)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ojmAxxs8Oa4)

[![](https://assets.sfstandard.com/image/994911177489/image_rlipk3k1s17d31t3h8pftt3i6d/-S3840x2560-FPNG)](https://sfstandard.com/pacific-standard-time/2025/12/17/gavin-newsom-interview-pst/)

Even at Walmart, builder roles are emerging. “If a year ago we sat down, we wouldn’t have any dedicated agent builders, but today we do,” the retail giant’s chief people officer, Donna Morris, said at Leading With AI. She added that all agent builder jobs have been filled internally, by both technical and nontechnical employees who were eager to start building with AI.

In San Francisco, some startups are explicitly posting builder roles, while others are simply redefining what they expect from employees.

Customer service platform Decagon is hiring an “[agent builder (opens in new tab)](https://jobs.ashbyhq.com/decagon/8c40fb7a-5f25-4112-a1df-f1c22b81042c)” to build AI agents for specific clients. Fintech startup SoFi is looking to fill a “[product builder (opens in new tab)](https://www.linkedin.com/jobs/view/product-builder-sandbox-at-sofi-4358795279/)” role to work with the engineer and product teams and “evangelize the benefits of AI-driven product development, management, and adoption.” And some employees at Applied Compute — a startup founded by former OpenAI employees that builds specialized AI models for companies — simply list “builder” as their role on LinkedIn.

“In startup land, ‘builder’ is in vogue now,” said Jeremiah Owyang, a general partner at Blitzscaling Ventures. “Everybody does so many tasks that look like a variety of roles, so it’s hard to pin a specific title on anybody.”

Some startups aren’t planning to jump on the “builder” hiring trend. Greptile, which is developing an AI code-review agent, is still posting roles for software engineers — but the company prioritizes candidates with strong product intuition who can take projects from idea to completion, said CEO Daksh Gupta.

The shift toward building is just getting started. Sal Khan, founder of the digital learning platform Khan Academy, has predicted that everyone is going to be a builder — and those who resist will be left behind.

“The people who are just waiting to get the spec … they’re going to have trouble,” Khan said at Leading With AI. “But the people who are like, ‘I’m going to go meet with the customer, and I can build it,’ I think they’re going to do great.”

**Do you want to check the best developer portfolios and website examples to gain inspiration?**

That’s when this collection comes in handy.

We analyzed 150 developers’ pages and tested their performance to create a list of the greatest 22.

This allowed us to come up with a collection where you can enjoy basic and minimalist design while being fun and engaging.

You’ll also find a lot of nice detailing (like custom cursors) that you may apply to your online presence.

Enjoy!

## Best Developer Portfolios For Your Inspiration

### 1\. [Devon Stank](https://devonstank.com/)

**Built with**: **[Squarespace](https://colorlib.com/go/squarespace)**

![devon stank personal website example](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/devon-stank-1.jpg)

Devon Stank is a stunning page with two features that make it stand out: the hero video background and the dark design.

Moreover, this is also a great [minimalist website example](https://colorlib.com/wp/minimalist-website-examples/) that creates a pleasant browsing and reading experience.

Additionally, Devon uses a top bar notification that you can close by pressing “x.”

**What stands out:** Use a video background to make your developer portfolio website more engaging.

Check out our dedicated [Squarespace website examples](https://colorlib.com/wp/squarespace-website-examples/) collection for more amazing designs.

### 2\. [Michael Mannucci](https://michaelmannucci.com/)

**Built with**: **[Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![michael mannucci personal website example](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/michael-mannucci.jpg)

Michael Mannucci’s site is the opposite of Devon’s, using light and vibrant colors. The only similarity the two pages have is the simplistic web design.

The page has a clean, transparent header with a call-to-action (CTA) button that takes you to the contact details.

Also, Michael Mannucci runs a [one-page website](https://colorlib.com/wp/one-page-websites/) structure, so all the details are only a few clicks away (but a floating header would come in very handy).

**What stands out:** Use a single-page layout to boost user experience.

You’ll also like peeking at these great [Webflow websites](https://colorlib.com/wp/webflow-websites/).

### 3\. [Diogo Correia](https://diogotc.com/)

**Built with**: **[Gatsby](https://www.gatsbyjs.com/)**

![diogo correia resume website](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/diogo-correia.jpg)

Diogo Correia has an exciting above-the-fold section with a particle effect background that spices things up nicely.

He also uses social media icons and sticky navigation in the hero section, making jumping from section to section much more comfortable.

Moreover, the timeline of work and experience reveals more details about Diogo and his skills.

**What stands out:** Use a sticky header/menu to navigate the website more easily.

### 4\. [Alex Naraghi](https://www.alexnaraghi.com/)

**Built with**: **[Squarespace](https://colorlib.com/go/squarespace)**

![alex naraghi resume website](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/alex-naraghi.jpg)

While some developers use images, videos and sliders above the fold, Alex Naraghi decided to keep it minimalist with text on a solid background. It works as a quick introduction, which is the most important thing.

Below the fold are some works and a CTA button to download Alex’s resume. The header and footer are clean, with only the main links and social buttons.

**What stands out:** There’s no need to complicate; use a text-only hero section.

### 5\. [Andrew McCarthy](https://andrevv.com/)

**Built with**: **[GitHub Pages](https://pages.github.com/)**

![andrew mccarthy resume website](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/andrew-mccarthy.jpg)

Andrew McCarthy is one of the more original developer portfolio websites we stumbled across. It has an infinite scroll with changing overlay elements, but here’s the catch: The sections repeat themselves repeatedly.

It’s tricky.

The header is basic and disappears on scroll but reappears once you start scrolling back to the top.

**What stands out:** Create a unique scrolling experience, like Andrew McCarthy!

### 6\. [La Playa](https://laplaya.studio/)

**Built with: [Squarespace](https://colorlib.com/go/squarespace)**

![la playa service website](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/la-playa.jpg)

La Playa is a developer’s portfolio example with a two-column grid layout and a sticky right sidebar with a drop-down info reveal. The grid’s cool feature is that it highlights the element on hover and dims the rest.

The header and the footer are plain, with some additional info (about the Canaries).

**What stands out:** Use a sticky sidebar to ensure links and information are always available.

What’s great is that you can create a simpler website using a [portfolio template for Squarespace](https://colorlib.com/wp/squarespace-portfolio-templates/) website builder.

### 7\. [Sharlee](https://itssharl.ee/fr)

**Built with: [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/)**

![sharlee developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/sharlee.jpg)

Sharlee’s engaging full-screen home page design has an animated background. It uses a day and light switcher and an overlayed hamburger menu with navigation and social media links. The header also has the option to switch languages from French to English (and vice versa).

**What stands out:** Use a dark/light mode switcher so visitors can choose how they want to view your website.

### 8\. [Brittany Chiang](https://brittanychiang.com/)

**Built with: [Gatsby](https://www.gatsbyjs.com/)**

![brittany chiang developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/brittany-chiang-1.jpg)

Brittany Chiang has a dark and minimalist [responsive web design](https://colorlib.com/wp/responsive-web-design/) with a title, text and a CTA above the fold.

It uses floating sidebar elements for social media and email and a disappearing/reappearing header.

Moreover, this developer portfolio has a one-page layout, so all the detail is only a few scrolls away.

**What stands out:** You can stand out from the rest by simply using a dark-ish design

### 9\. [Lauren Waller](https://www.lauren-waller.com/)

**Built with: [Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![lauren waller developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/lauren-waller-developer-portfolio.jpg)

Lauren Waller’s home page is plain and simple, with a short bio and large menu links that respond on hover.

There is no header and footer, keeping the minimalistic level at an all-time high. However, there’s a neat sidebar with links to social media (or home when viewing internal pages).

**What stands out:** Do you want to keep your developer portfolio page extra clean? Skip the header and footer.

Remember, we also have a full list of the greatest [clean websites](https://colorlib.com/wp/clean-websites/) you need to check out.

### 10\. [Adenekan Wonderful](https://www.codewonders.dev/)

**Built with: [Next.js](https://nextjs.org/)**

![adenekan wonderful developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/adenekan-wonderful.jpg)

Similar to Lauren’s site, Adenekan Wonderful also has a text-only home page with some detailing to make it more captivating.

The two-sentence bio includes all the necessary (navigation) links, an email, and a link to the about me page.

**What stands out:** While pure minimalism works really well for web design, make it more exciting with a simple animation.

### 11\. [Gift Egwuenu](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)

**Built with: [Gridsome](https://gridsome.org/)**

![gift egwuenu developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/gift-egwuenu-developer-portfolio.jpg)

Gift Egwuenu has a modern, text-heavy online developer portfolio page with an excellent animated profile picture that disrupts the seriousness.

What’s unique about this website is the framed pink border, which becomes even more visible when you turn on the dark mode view. The pink color is also used in other elements and details, adding a nice branded touch.

**What stands out:** Add personal touches and branding to create a pleasant web atmosphere.

### 12\. [Kenneth Jimmy](https://kenjimmy.xyz/)

**Built with: [Nuxt.js](https://nuxtjs.org/)**

![kenneth jimmy developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/kenneth-jimmy-developer-portfolio.jpg)

Kenneth Jimmy’s developer portfolio site is framed and boxed, with a unique scrolling experience that you must try to understand. It easily immerses you in the content and familiarizes you with Kenneth’s work.

The header features site navigation, email and a dark/light mode switcher. Kenneth also strategically uses a CTA button above the fold to contact him immediately.

**What stands out:** Instead of a full-width web layout, use a boxed or framed one.

### 13\. [Tamal Sen](https://tamalsen.dev/)

**Built with: [Elementor](https://colorlib.com/out/elementor)**

![tamal sen developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/tamal-sen.jpg)

Tamal Senj’s website has a nice “developer’s” touch that blends well with the dark-ish look. At the same time, the colored details elevate the scrolling experience, which would also be amazing without it.

The floating header effortlessly navigates through the single-page layout, while the back-to-top button lets you jump to the top with a single click.

**What stands out:** Adding a back-to-top button can improve your developer portfolio’s UX.

Do you plan on using WordPress for your [personal website](https://colorlib.com/wp/personal-websites/)? Then check our extensive [Elementor review](https://colorlib.com/wp/elementor-review/).

### 14\. [Matthew Williams](http://findmatthew.com/)

**Built with: [GitHub Pages](https://pages.github.com/)**

![matthew william developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/matthew-williams-developer-portfolio.jpg)

There’s one thing that most of these websites have in common: their design leans more toward minimalism.

Matthew Williams has a full-screen hero section with animated background, text and a CTA button. The navigation bar only appears below the fold and sticks to the screen.

Moreover, the site has a filterable portfolio, animated skillbars, a basic contact form and social media icons in the footer.

**What stands out:** Do you have multiple styles of projects to showcase? Use a filterable/categorized portfolio.

### 15\. [Stereo Creative](https://stereocreative.com/)

**Built with: [Craft CMS](https://craftcms.com/)**

![stereo creative developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/stereo-creative-developer-portfolio.jpg)

Stereo Creative is another great proof that a strong message above the fold on a solid background can work really well.

While this [website starts simple](https://colorlib.com/wp/simple-websites/), it then hits you with a cool animation that almost acts as a short promotional video.

Furthermore, Stereo Creative’s page has a header that disappears on the scroll and reappears when you scroll back to the top.

The home page also features a few of their works and a clients’ list, which builds proof of quality.

**What stands out:** Have you worked with some big brands and companies? Add them to your website as a reference.

### 16\. [Koysor Abdul](https://www.koysor.me/)

**Built with: [Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![koysor abdul developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/koysor-abdul.jpg)

The pleasant scrolling experience makes you hope Koysor Abdul’s website never ends. Okay, it’s also because of the excellent work that he showcases for your viewing pleasure.

This is another terrific developer portfolio website example built with cleanness and great UX in mind.

**What stands out:** Link your portfolio works to live projects, so potential clients can review them first-hand.

### 17\. [Lars Olson](https://www.lars-olson.com/)

**Built with: [Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![lars olson developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/lars-olson.jpg)

Lars Olson knows how to make a developer’s page entertaining and engaging with a cool selection of catchy details and fun elements. And the unique hover effects activate it some more.

It’s a semi-single-layout website with external links to social media and resume. Moreover, the portfolio opens projects on individual pages where you can find an in-depth presentation.

**What stands out:** Let your fun and bubbly personality shine through your web design.

### 18\. [Niall Mc Dermott](https://niallmcdermott.webflow.io/)

**Built with: [Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![niall mc dermott developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/niall-mc-dermott.jpg)

What we like about Niall Mc Dermott’s website is that even though it’s built on Webflow’s free plan, it’s still very next-level-ish.

The special thing about this developer portfolio is the split-screen design, with static left and dynamic right parts. This creates an interesting scrolling experience, but you can also use the hamburger menu to find other details and information about Niall.

**What stands out:** A [free website builder](https://colorlib.com/wp/top-free-website-builders/) doesn’t always mean half-baked products; sometimes, you cannot use a custom domain name (or the functionality is slightly limited).

### 19\. **[Edgar Deiner](https://www.edgardeiner.com/)**

**Built with: [Webflow](https://colorlib.com/out/webflow)**

![edgar deiner developer portfolio](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/edgar-deiner.jpg)

Edgar Deiner also has a split-screen design but without the sticky part. However, Edgar’s website uses a sidebar navigation/header that floats, ensuring you always have access to the menu and social media links.

A black and yellow color scheme elevates the atmosphere, while the extra white space calls for likable readability.

**What stands out:** Are you tired of traditional headers and menus? Use a vertical sidebar version like Edgar.

### 20\. [Developer Tim Gesemann](https://www.tim-gesemann.dev/)

**Made with: [Svelte](https://svelte.dev/)**

![Tim Gesemann - developer portfolio example](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/developer-tim-gesemann.jpg)

Tim is yet another developer who turns coffee into code. He uses Svelte, a UI builder for web apps, and hosts it on Vercel. These two are the most developer things right now. The portfolio features a clean and minimalistic design with a prominent project section followed by a creative timeline of his work experience. He is working for Volkswagen as a Fullstack Software Engineer, likely working on implementing even more things into the touchscreen in your car.

**What stands out:** The website offers a mix of creativity and simplicity, making it an excellent example for everyone creating their developer portfolio.

### 21\. [Cassie Evans](https://cassie.codes/)

**Built with**: **[Custom](https://cassie.codes/)**

![cassie evans developer portfolio example](https://colorlib.com/wp/wp-content/uploads/sites/2/cassie-codes.jpg)

Cassie Evans’ portfolio at cassie.codes is instantly delightful — a custom illustrated desk scene with a monitor, lamp, plants, and books creates a cozy developer workspace. The split layout pairs this playful illustration with bold typography: “I like making fun, interactive things with code.”

The navigation (writing, speaking, workshop, playing) reflects a developer who does more than just code — she teaches, speaks, and experiments. The mint green and gray color scheme feels fresh and approachable, perfectly matching her creative coding focus.

**What stands out:** Custom illustrations of a workspace add personality and warmth that stock photos cannot match, making developer portfolios feel uniquely personal.

If you're trying to break into AI engineering in 2026 or level up from where you are, here are 5 portfolio projects that will genuinely make a difference when you're sitting in front of a hiring manager.

I'm not talking about tutorial projects where you deploy a basic chatbot. I'm talking about work that makes someone look at your GitHub and say, "this person actually understands how production AI systems work.

The 5 Projects:
Project 1: Production RAG Application
Build a domain-specific "Ask My Docs" system with hybrid retrieval (BM25 + vector search), cross-encoder reranking, citation enforcement, and a CI-gated evaluation pipeline. This is the most common pattern in enterprise AI right now.

Project 2: Local SLM App with Ollama
Run models entirely offline. Benchmark inference performance. Compare 3 models on the same hardware. Document the quality-vs-speed tradeoffs. Privacy, latency, and cost constraints are real- show you understand them.

Project 3: Monitoring & Observability
Add tracing, latency tracking (p50/p95), cost-per-request, and quality metrics to your RAG system. Build regression gating into CI. This is 70% of production AI work that nobody puts in their portfolio.

Project 4: Fine-Tuning with LoRA & DPO
Fine-tune for a specific task (JSON extraction or tool-calling). Use LoRA/QLoRA for efficient training. Add preference tuning with DPO. Show before-and-after metrics with actual numbers.

Project 5: Real-Time Multimodal Application
Build a voice assistant or streaming pipeline. Decompose end-to-end latency into a detailed budget. Add graceful degradation and timeout handling. Show you understand real-time systems.

These 5 projects tell a cohesive story: you build AI systems that are reliable, measurable, and production-ready.
